items.q: Why do meanders migrate across a floodplain?
items.correct: Faster water on the outside of the bend causes erosion (river cliff); slower water on the inside causes deposition (slip-off slope) — over time the channel shifts laterally.
items.misconception: Meanders form because rivers flow around obstacles like rocks.
items.random_wrong: Meanders form because the river is going downhill very steeply.
items.why: Meanders form on gentle gradients, not steep ones, and self-organise rather than dodging obstacles. Students project an intuitive 'water goes around things' model onto a fluid dynamic process.

items.correct: Through weathering of parent rock, decomposition of organic matter, biological activity, climate influence and time — a slow process taking hundreds to thousands of years.
items.why: Soil is a complex mix of mineral and organic matter, water, air and living organisms. Students reduce it to ground rock, missing the crucial biological component.

items.q: Why might managed retreat be more sustainable than building a sea wall?
items.correct: Sea walls are expensive, deflect erosion elsewhere and reflect wave energy worsening beach loss; managed retreat works with natural processes, creates habitat and is cheaper long-term.
items.misconception: Sea walls always solve coastal erosion problems permanently.
items.random_wrong: Managed retreat means letting all coastal towns disappear into the sea.
items.why: Sea walls protect one stretch but cause terminal scour and sediment starvation downdrift. Managed retreat selectively allows the coast to adjust — controversial socially but ecologically sound.
The answer examiners want is: El Niño-Southern Oscillation. “Equatorial Northern Sea Outflow” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which phase suppresses upwelling along South America's Pacific coast?
items.why: Correct answer: El Niño. “La Niña” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which year did the very strong 1997- El Niño peak (year started)?
items.why: The real figure is 1997. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
